title: Deckle edge and waterleaf
subtitle: Controlling the natural feathered edge of a handmade sheet
excerpt: The deckle edge is the characteristic ragged border of a handmade paper sheet, formed where the wet furnish seeps under the deckle frame. A waterleaf is an unsized sheet that still carries its natural deckle edge intact. Understanding how to preserve or reduce the deckle edge is part of sheet finishing.
sourceNotes: Dard Hunter, Old Papermaking (1923, public domain), chapter on mould and deckle construction and the natural feathered edge. Internet Archive scan available.

glossaryTerms.definition: The uneven feathered border of a handmade sheet, formed where pulp seeps under the deckle frame during sheet-pulling.
A handmade sheet that has not been surface-sized, retaining its natural absorbency and deckle edges.
Applying a gelatin or starch solution to a finished waterleaf to reduce ink absorbency and improve the writing surface.

content.text: on all four sides unless it is trimmed away. Whether to keep or reduce the deckle edge depends on how the sheet will be used. A bookbinding endpaper often needs clean edges. A sheet sold as art paper usually keeps all four deckle edges to show it was made by hand.
Standard papermaking setup: vat, mould and deckle, cotton linter furnish, felts, press.
For a clean edge: a metal ruler and bone folder to tear against after the sheet dries.

When the mould is lifted from the vat, the wet furnish is held between the mould surface and the deckle frame. Where the deckle presses against the mould, it contains the pulp cleanly. But the deckle cannot press perfectly at every point: at the corners and at any warp in the deckle edge, pulp seeps under and forms a thin feathered extension. This is the deckle edge.
Keep the deckle pressed firmly against the mould throughout the pull. Press with the thumbs at the corners, where seepage is worst.
Use a slightly thicker furnish: thicker pulp seeps less readily under the deckle than a very thin slurry.
After drying, tear the sheet against a metal ruler for a clean edge. A torn-against-ruler edge is not the same as a cut edge: it has a slight feathered quality but is consistent across the sheet.
What a waterleaf is used for

absorbs liquid immediately on contact. This makes it ideal for soft watercolour washes (the pigment sinks into the fibre structure) and for paste-decorated paper (the paste absorbs evenly). For calligraphy or printmaking, the sheet needs
